---
title: "Lists"
description: "Store, access, slice, update, copy, and loop over ordered mutable collections."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Store, access, slice, update, copy, and loop over ordered mutable collections.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Lists** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 4 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Lists** — Store ordered collections that can grow and change.
- Section 2: **List Indexing and Slicing** — Access one list item or a smaller list by position.
- Section 3: **List Methods** — Use common list methods such as append, remove, sort, and pop.
- Section 4: **List Copying and Aliasing** — Understand when two names refer to the same list and how to make copies.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
A list is one object that stores items in order. Names can point to that same
list, or to a separate copy.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  tasks["name<br/>tasks"] --> list["list object<br/>index 0: 'predict'<br/>index 1: 'practice'<br/>index 2: 'reflect'"]
  alias["name<br/>alias"] --> list
  copy["name<br/>copy"] --> copylist["separate list object<br/>index 0: 'predict'<br/>index 1: 'practice'"]
  list --> slice["slice tasks[1:3]<br/>new list with indexes 1 and 2"]
```

This diagram explains why aliasing can surprise beginners: two names can point to
one mutable list object.
:::

## Section 1: Lists

Store ordered collections that can grow and change.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A list is a row of numbered lockers. You can open locker 0, replace what is inside, or
add another locker at the end.
:::

### Mental model

A list is a mutable ordered container; order matters and items can be replaced, added,
or removed.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
tasks = ["read", "practice"]
tasks.append("reflect")
tasks[0] = "predict"
print(tasks)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `tasks = ["read", "practice"]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `tasks.append("reflect")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `tasks[0] = "predict"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(tasks)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Using an index that does not exist raises IndexError. Check len(items) before reaching
past the end.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-lists",
  "title": "Lists checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Lists?",
      "options": [
        "A list is a mutable ordered container; order matters and items can be replaced, added, or removed.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A list is a mutable ordered container; order matters and items can be replaced, added, or removed."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A list is a row of numbered lockers. You can open locker 0, replace what is inside, or add another locker at the end."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: List Indexing and Slicing

Access one list item or a smaller list by position.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Indexing is pointing to one book on a shelf; slicing is taking a section of neighboring
books.
:::

### Mental model

Indexing selects one item; slicing copies a range of items into a new list.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d"]
print(letters[0])
print(letters[-1])
print(letters[1:3])
```

### Walkthrough

1. `letters = ["a", "b", "c", "d"]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print(letters[0])` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `print(letters[-1])` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(letters[1:3])` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
The stop position in a slice is excluded. items[1:3] returns positions 1 and 2, not 3.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-list-indexing-slicing",
  "title": "List Indexing and Slicing checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes List Indexing and Slicing?",
      "options": [
        "Indexing selects one item; slicing copies a range of items into a new list.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Indexing selects one item; slicing copies a range of items into a new list."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Indexing is pointing to one book on a shelf; slicing is taking a section of neighboring books."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: List Methods

Use common list methods such as append, remove, sort, and pop.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

List methods are tools hanging on the list itself: append adds, sort orders, pop removes
and returns.
:::

### Mental model

Methods are actions attached to objects; list methods change or inspect a list.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
numbers = [3, 1, 2]
numbers.sort()
last = numbers.pop()
print(numbers)
print(last)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `numbers = [3, 1, 2]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `numbers.sort()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `last = numbers.pop()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(numbers)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `print(last)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Some methods change the list in place and return None. Do not write numbers =
numbers.sort().
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-list-methods",
  "title": "List Methods checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes List Methods?",
      "options": [
        "Methods are actions attached to objects; list methods change or inspect a list.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Methods are actions attached to objects; list methods change or inspect a list."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: List methods are tools hanging on the list itself: append adds, sort orders, pop removes and returns."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 4: List Copying and Aliasing

Understand when two names refer to the same list and how to make copies.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Aliasing is two people holding strings tied to the same balloon: moving one string moves
the same balloon.
:::

### Mental model

Aliasing happens when multiple names point to one mutable object.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
original = ["a", "b"]
alias = original
copy = original.copy()
alias.append("c")
print(original)
print(copy)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `original = ["a", "b"]` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `alias = original` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `copy = original.copy()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `alias.append("c")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `print(original)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
6. Continue the same process for the remaining lines, one line at a time.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Assigning one list to another name does not copy the list. Use .copy() or list slicing
when you need a separate list.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-structures-list-copying-aliasing",
  "title": "List Copying and Aliasing checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes List Copying and Aliasing?",
      "options": [
        "Aliasing happens when multiple names point to one mutable object.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Aliasing happens when multiple names point to one mutable object."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Aliasing is two people holding strings tied to the same balloon: moving one string moves the same balloon."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Lists**: A list is a mutable ordered container; order matters and items can be replaced, added,
or removed.
- **List Indexing and Slicing**: Indexing selects one item; slicing copies a range of items into a new list.
- **List Methods**: Methods are actions attached to objects; list methods change or inspect a list.
- **List Copying and Aliasing**: Aliasing happens when multiple names point to one mutable object.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Python data structures tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/datastructures.html
- Built-in types: https://docs.python.org/3/library/stdtypes.html
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
